m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the 'this' pointer in C++ primarily used for?

Options.OptionText: To point to any random object
To point to the next object
To point to the current object
To point to the previous object

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'this' pointer is used to point to the current object within a class method.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about the 'this' pointer is false?

Options.OptionText: 'this' pointer is implicitly available within non-static member functions of a class.
'this' pointer can be used in static member functions of a class.
'this' pointer is automatically passed to a non-static member function for the object call.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions do not have a 'this' pointer because they are not bound to any specific object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a method needs to return the current instance of the class, which of the following is the preferred way?
m_Item1.Explanation: Returning *this will return the current object by reference, which is a common practice in method chaining.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What type of pointer is 'this' in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'this' pointer is a non-const pointer that points to the current instance of the class, allowing member functions to access non-static members.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider the code: class Sample { void func(); ... };. What is the type of 'this' pointer inside func()?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inside a member function, 'this' is a pointer of type (ClassName*), pointing to the current object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why is the 'this' pointer not allowed in a static member function?

Options.OptionText: Static functions are unrelated to objects.
Static functions have a different syntax.
Static functions cannot access member functions.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions do not have access to the 'this' pointer because they are not invoked on any object instance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a member function of class MyClass, what does the expression 'this == &obj' signify?

Options.OptionText: 'this' and 'obj' point to the same object
'obj' is a friend of the object pointed by 'this'

m_Item1.Explanation: The expression checks whether the 'this' pointer and 'obj' are pointing to the same memory location, meaning they refer to the same object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can 'this' be used to solve the name conflict between member variable and parameter name?

Options.OptionText: By using a different class name alias
By using 'this' to explicitly specify the member

m_Item1.Explanation: 'this->' can be used to distinguish between a class member variable and a parameter with the same name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is correctly demonstrated use of the 'this' pointer?
m_Item1.Explanation: Returning '*this' is the correct way to return an instance by reference using the 'this' pointer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if you try to modify 'this' inside a constant member function?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a constant member function, 'this' is treated as a pointer to a constant object, hence modifying it causes a compile-time error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which of the following scenarios is using 'this' pointer mandatory?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'this' pointer becomes necessary when there is ambiguity, such as when member variables and parameters share the same name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'this' keyword point to in a class constructor?

Options.OptionText: The address of the current object being constructed

m_Item1.Explanation: In a constructor, 'this' points to the memory location of the object currently being constructed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the result of 'this->member' code in C++?

Options.OptionText: Accesses the member of the current object

m_Item1.Explanation: 'this->member' is used to access the member of the object pointed by 'this'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement about static data members in C++ is correct?

Options.OptionText: They maintain a separate copy for each object.
They are initialized automatically to zero.
They can only be accessed from static member functions.
They are accessed using the -> operator.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static data members are initialized to zero by default if not explicitly initialized.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How many copies of a static member function exist in terms of instance objects?

Options.OptionText: Equal to the number of objects.
Only one copy, shared across all instances.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions are shared across all instances and not tied to any specific object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the correct way to define a static data member in a class?

Options.OptionText: Inside the class definition, similar to non-static members.
Outside the class definition using the scope resolution operator.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static data members are declared in the class but defined outside the class using the scope resolution operator.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if you try to access a non-static member from within a static member function?

Options.OptionText: Compiles successfully and works as expected.
Compiles but throws a runtime error.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions do not have access to the 'this' pointer, hence cannot access non-static members directly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why might you use a static data member in a C++ class?

Options.OptionText: To ensure every object has its own copy of the member.
To share a class-level data across all instances.
To achieve polymorphism with derived classes.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static data members are used to share data across all instances of a class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can static member functions be virtual in C++?

Options.OptionText: Yes, but only in specific situations.
No, because static functions do not involve an object instance.
No, unless they are declared inline.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions cannot be virtual because they don't operate on a specific instance of a class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a reason to use a static member function?

Options.OptionText: To compute class-wide operations that do not require instance-specific data.
To store data only relevant to one instance.
To dynamically allocate memory for objects.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions are used when the operation is related to the class as a whole rather than any specific instance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When is a static data member constructed in a C++ class?

Options.OptionText: When the first object of the class is created.
When the class is first referenced.
When the main function is executed.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static data members are created when they are first referenced in the program, not when an object is created.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where in memory are static data members typically allocated?
m_Item1.Explanation: Static data members are allocated in the static segment of memory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a static member function access static data members?

Options.OptionText: Yes, it can access all members.
Yes, it can only access static data members.
No, it cannot access any data members.
No, unless they are passed as parameters.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions can only access static data members since they are class-level.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to declare a static data member inside a class?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'static' keyword is used to declare a static data member inside a class in C++.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is required to invoke a static member function?

Options.OptionText: An object instance of the class.
A pointer to an object instance.
Class name and scope resolution operator.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions are invoked using the class name and the scope resolution operator.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When is a static data member initialized if not explicitly done?

Options.OptionText: The first time a class function is called.
When the first object is created.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static data members are initialized before the main function executes, provided they're used in the program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Is it mandatory to define a static data member outside the class?

Options.OptionText: Yes, unless it's a const static member of integral type.

m_Item1.Explanation: A static data member must be defined outside the class unless it's a const static member of an integral type initialized in the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of a static member function in a utility class?

Options.OptionText: To perform operations specific to a single instance.
To perform tasks not associated with object state.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions in a utility class perform tasks that do not depend on the state of individual class instances.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can you declare a static function inside a private section of a class?

Options.OptionText: No, static functions must be public.
Yes, but it can only be accessed from friend classes.
Yes, it behaves like any other private function.
No, static functions have to be globally accessible.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static functions can be declared in the private section of a class and behave like any other private member.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is a static data member initialized in the class definition?

Options.OptionText: Simply by assigning a value in the class definition.
Using the 'static' keyword outside the class with scope resolution.
It must be initialized within a constructor.

m_Item1.Explanation: A static data member is declared within the class and initialized outside using the class scope and 'static' keyword.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can static member functions be overloaded?

Options.OptionText: Yes, they can be overloaded like any other functions.
Yes, but with a different signature than non-static functions.
No, unless they're defined as inline functions.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions can be overloaded like other member functions based on their signatures.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the impact of static member functions on the size of instances of the class?

Options.OptionText: It increases the size of each object.
It decreases the size of each object.
It does not affect the size of class instances.
It affects only when the function is called.

m_Item1.Explanation: Static member functions do not affect the size of class objects, as they are not tied to instances.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following declarations in a class is incorrect?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'void static update();' is incorrect because it incorrectly places 'static' after the return type instead of before the function name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The implicit argument passed to a member function of a C++ class is called as
m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, The implicit argument passed to a member function of class is called as "this" pointer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The fields in the class in c++ program are by default
m_Item1.Explanation: The fields of class are private by default in c++ program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Identify the correct statement.

Options.OptionText: Namespace is used to group class, objects and functions.
Namespace is used to mark the beginning of the program.
Namespace is used to seperate the class, objects.

m_Item1.Explanation: Namespace is used to group class, objects and functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ___________ member variable is initialized to zero when the first object of its class is created .
m_Item1.Explanation: As the variables declared as static are initialized only once as they are allocated space in separate static storage so, the static variables in a class are shared by the objects. and its initial value is zero.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A class can be
m_Item1.Explanation: Class can be vitual ,generic,inline so all of above opetions are true.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The implicit argument passed to a member function of a C++ class is called as
m_Item1.Explanation: The this pointer holds the address of current object, in simple words you can say that this pointer points to the current object of the class. And it is passed to a class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following concepts provides facility of using object of one class inside definition of another class?
m_Item1.Explanation: Composition is a design principle in object-oriented programming where one class is composed of one or more objects of other classes, implying a "has-a" relationship. This allows a class to reuse the functionality of other classes by containing instances of these classes as member variables.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which one of the following is not the property of constructor?

Options.OptionText: They should be declared in the public section.
They de not have return type.

m_Item1.Explanation: We can not create Virtual constructor

m_Item1.QuestionText: If abstract class is inherited by derived class, then_______________ .

Options.OptionText: Derived class should provide definition for all the pure virtual functions
Derived class also become abstract if fails to implement pure virtual functions
Objects of derived class can’t be created if it fails to implement pure virtual functions

m_Item1.Explanation: If abstract class is inherited by derived class, then Derived class should provide definition for all the pure virtual functions otherwise derived class also become abstract if fails to implement pure virtual functions. If derived class is also become abstract then you cannot create object of derived class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following  type of class allows only one object of it to be created?
m_Item1.Explanation: A singleton class is a special type of class in object-oriented programming which can have only one object or instance at a time. In other words, we can instantiate only one instance of the singleton class.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be output of following code?
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;
class A
{
    int a;
   public:
    A(){
        a = 0;
    }
    static void show(){
        a++;
        cout<<a;
    }
};
class B: public A
{
   public:
 
};
int main(int argc, char const *argv[])
{
    B b;
    b.show();
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Non-static members of class cannot be used inside a static functions of class.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be output of following program?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
class Program
{
    int *p;
    public:
    Program(int xx, char ch)
    {
        p  = new int();
        *p = xx + int(ch);
        cout<< *p;
    }
    ~Program()
    {
        delete p;
    }
};
int main()
{
    Program obj(15, 'A');
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: The program will print the output 80.
The program will print the output 112.
The program will print the output garbage value.
he program will report compile time error.

m_Item1.Explanation: Here the character is converted into int i.e. 'A'=65 which is added to the xx value 15 and thus we get the output 80.
